examples.title: Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) Example
examples.description: Demonstrates how an interrupt service routine handles keyboard input
examples.code:
ALGORITHM KeyboardISR
BEGIN
  SAVE_CPU_STATE()
  READ_KEYBOARD_BUFFER()
  STORE_CHARACTER_IN_QUEUE(currentChar)
  SIGNAL_KEYBOARD_CONTROLLER()
  UPDATE_KEYBOARD_STATUS()
  RESTORE_CPU_STATE()
  RETURN_FROM_INTERRUPT()
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the structure of an interrupt service routine. When a keyboard interrupt occurs, the CPU saves its current state, processes the keyboard input by reading from the keyboard buffer, stores the character in a queue for later use, signals the keyboard controller that processing is complete, and then restores the CPU state before returning to the interrupted program.

examples.title: DMA Transfer Setup Procedure
examples.description: Shows how to configure DMA controller for a block data transfer
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SetupDMATransfer
BEGIN
  INPUT: sourceAddress, destAddress, transferLength, deviceID
  WRITE_TO_DMA_REG(DMA_CONTROL, STOP_DMA)
  WRITE_TO_DMA_REG(DMA_SOURCE, sourceAddress)
  WRITE_TO_DMA_REG(DMA_DEST, destAddress)
  WRITE_TO_DMA_REG(DMA_COUNT, transferLength)
  WRITE_TO_DMA_REG(DMA_MODE, WRITE_MODE)
  WRITE_TO_DMA_REG(DMA_COMMAND, ENABLE_DMA)
  SIGNAL_DEVICE_FOR_TRANSFER(deviceID)
  RETURN
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows the steps to configure a DMA controller. The CPU programs the DMA controller by writing source address, destination address, transfer count, and transfer mode. After enabling DMA, the controller handles the actual data transfer independently, allowing the CPU to perform other tasks.

examples.title: Cache Lookup Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates cache hit/miss detection and data retrieval
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CacheAccess
BEGIN
  INPUT: memoryAddress
  SET index = EXTRACT_INDEX(memoryAddress, CACHE_SIZE)
  SET tag = EXTRACT_TAG(memoryAddress, CACHE_SIZE)
  SET cacheLine = CACHE[index]
  
  IF cacheLine.valid AND cacheLine.tag == tag THEN
    SET offset = EXTRACT_OFFSET(memoryAddress)
    RETURN cacheLine.data[offset]
  ELSE
    CALL FETCH_FROM_MAIN_MEMORY(memoryAddress)
    UPDATE_CACHE_LINE(index, tag, memoryData)
    RETURN memoryData
  END IF
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm demonstrates how cache memory is accessed. First, the cache index and tag are extracted from the memory address. If the cached line is valid and tags match (cache hit), data is returned from cache. On cache miss, data is fetched from main memory, the cache is updated with new data, and then returned.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for hit, O(n) for miss

examples.title: Bootstrap Loader Process
examples.description: Shows the initialization sequence when computer powers on
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BootstrapProcess
BEGIN
  POWER_ON_RESET()
  SET_CPU_TO_KNOWN_STATE()
  EXECUTE_POST()
  INITIALIZE_BASIC_HARDWARE()
  
  READ_BOOT_SECTOR_FROM_MEDIA()
  LOAD_BOOT_LOADER_INTO_MEMORY()
  TRANSFER_CONTROL_TO_BOOT_LOADER()
  
  READ_OS_CONFIGURATION()
  LOAD_KERNEL_INTO_MEMORY()
  INITIALIZE_MEMORY_MANAGEMENT()
  SETUP_INTERRUPT_DESCRIPTORS()
  START_SCHEDULER()
  
  LAUNCH_INIT_PROCESS()
  DISPLAY_LOGIN_PROMPT()
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows the complete bootstrap process from power-on to operating system loading. It includes power-on self test (POST), hardware initialization, loading the boot sector, transferring control to the boot loader, loading the OS kernel, setting up memory management and interrupts, and finally launching the first process and displaying the login prompt.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) based on system initialization tasks
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for kernel and system structures
